title: Inert Prime Area Quantization PASS: p=7, k=2 (mod 49) — second inert prime
_fixture_purpose: Second-inert-prime fixture. p=7, k=2, modulus=49. This is a genuinely different prime from the p=3 anchor and generalization — it exercises a different GF(p^2) structure. The Legendre symbol (5/7)=-1 (since 5^3≡6≡-1 mod 7), confirming 7 is inert in Z[phi]. Forbidden = {7,14,21,28,35,42} = {r : v_7(r)=1}. This proves the theorem is not an artifact of p=3 but holds for the full inert-prime family. The spectrum has 43 elements out of 49; |forbidden|=6 = p*(p-1)/1 = 7-1 = 6 (for k=2, |forbidden| = p-1 * p^(k-1) / ... = p*(p-1)? No: {7,14,21,28,35,42} = 6 values = p-1 = 6 for k=2. Correct: |{r: 0<r<p^2, v_p(r)=1}| = p-1.)

theorem_claim.statement: For p=7 (inert in Z[phi]) and k=2: Im(f) = {r in Z/49Z : v_7(r) != 1}. Forbidden = {r : 7|r but 49∤r} = {7,14,21,28,35,42}.
theorem_claim.forbidden_description: Residues r in {0..48} with v_7(r)=1: the six multiples of 7 that are not multiples of 49. For inert p, no element of Z[phi]/p^2 Z[phi] has norm ≡ pu (gcd(u,p)=1), because the norm of any element divisible by the maximal ideal of GR(p^2,2) must be divisible by p^2.

validation_checks.description: p=7 is inert in Z[phi]: Legendre(5,7)=-1

validation_checks.details: actual_spectrum (43 values) == claimed; all r in {0..48} \ {7,14,21,28,35,42} achieved
